author: Coffee Nodes
msg:
I think rewards were "boosted" early on to kickstart the network, especially with delays and the expectation that more tasks would already be live by now. What matters to me is whether the system is sustainable. If it is, it will grow; just need time.

Paying out large amounts to big farms doesn’t make much sense if the data collected isn’t valuable. That model doesn’t hold long. And yeah it's annoying. I bought four boxes, paying for 4x ISPs, and seeing around $3/day — which means I won't see a ROI on that stuff for some time.

But zooming out, when more tasks become available, I do hope it will dwarf the rewards we see now (shouldn't be too difficult if telecom is as lucrative as it was sold to us)

author: Jaba - Unityfarmers

author: capri
msg:
Let’s be honest and speak openly. Otherwise, we’re not only misleading ourselves, but also everyone around us.

They strongly pushed and incentivized us to buy nodes — encouraging many of us to go for at least 3 nodes, meaning a minimum of 600 licenses. We trusted them and invested.

Now the real question is: what are we supposed to do with these licenses? Keep them as souvenirs to remind us how naive we were — or try to lease them under conditions that simply don’t make sense?

Who is realistically going to lease them, running devices 24/7 with SIM cards, draining battery, consuming internet — while earning rewards that don’t even cover the operating costs? And even if they barely do, convincing people to participate under these conditions is extremely difficult.

We tried to be proactive. As a “solution,” many of us invested even more in farming setups to support the project and help it succeed — hoping we could at least cover our costs. But instead of mutual support to sustain the ecosystem, the rewards were reduced, making it impossible to cover even basic operating expenses.

We bought nodes (licenses), devices, SIM cards, internet subscriptions, routers, and networking equipment — and we invested our valuable time.

This is not just frustrating — it feels deeply unfair, misleading, and completely unsustainable.

author: Rick Middleton

author: MikiRoma87 Miki
msg: the thing is, As per what i understood since the beginning, also by knowing this project from a different platform thanks to @coffee_wmtx , then watching videos of DadGoneCrypto, every node owner needs to find a way to promote his licenses be it youtube, instagram etc etc, without expecting them to all go on the market place (not at this point anyways...) obviously if you have a channel which already has subscribers or an audience that knows you, it might be easier, not so easy with current conditions for everyone else, but it's not a passive income at all.

author: MikiRoma87 Miki
msg: at the cost of being unpopular with people that own many nodes, and perhaps were expecting to receive rewards without any effort what broke the market even more in my view, are the 90/10, 91/9, 95/5 licenses that were available on the marketplace and I'm so glad they capped it at 50%.

author: Dave
msg:
Gemini telling me:

A mobile device's IP address on a cellular network (4G/5G) typically rotates every few hours to 24 hours due to DHCP lease renewals by the provider. However, IP changes can occur more frequently—sometimes within minutes—if the user moves between cell towers, toggles Airplane Mode, or if the carrier manages high traffic.

author: Daano
msg: Are they even allowed to check mac address in regards to GDPR/privacy rules?

author: Rick Middleton
msg: I think GDPR applies to personal informaton... so a mac address is not likely to be covered.. it is a device id.. not of a person.. just my guess though.

author: Daniel 🇷🇴
msg: Usually it applies because it's unique

author: Daniel 🇷🇴
msg: So you can identify  a person

author: Rick Middleton
msg:
By the looks of things it is not a problem if it is fully anonymised - so there is no way to link it to a person.  If that was the case GDPR does not apply..

But given that for our purposes of security certain other data might be recorded it is possible the standard GDPR regulations should apply...  and tracking  crooks is definitely an allowed purpose...

author: Rick Middleton
msg: In short YES we are allowed to check

author: Rick Middleton
msg: We just need to store the data securely
